nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; tends to hover above coral heads, inside caves, and in shaded reef areas at depths of roughly 3–46 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on small zooplankton, copepods, brine shrimp, frozen mysis shrimp, and other small meaty preparations. Generally advised to offer small amounts several times daily; frozen vitamin-enriched foods are commonly recommended.

summary: A tiny, translucent Caribbean reef goby with orange facial markings. Generally considered reef-safe and peaceful; tends to hover in open water above coral heads and caves. Captive-bred specimens are sometimes available.
careNotes: The Masked Goby is generally considered one of the easier nano reef fish to keep. In the wild it tends to occur in schools near coral columns and caves throughout the tropical western Atlantic (Florida, Bermuda, Bahamas, and northern South America). In an aquarium, it is generally advised to provide plenty of live rock with caves and overhangs for shelter, as individuals naturally gravitate toward shaded areas and hover above the bottom rather than resting on substrate. While it can be kept singly, groups of 3 or more tend to show more natural schooling behavior; a tank of at least 75 L is recommended to comfortably house a small group. This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite (female-first sex change); dominant individuals will change sex to male. Captive breeding has been reported by specialty breeders such as ORA, with spawning observed as early as 100 days post-hatching, though breeding in a home aquarium is generally considered moderately challenging. A tightly-fitted lid is advisable due to the small size of this fish and the general jumping tendency of gobies. Temperature sources show some disagreement: LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C) while FishBase wild data suggests 24.9–28°C; the range used here (23.5–27°C) represents an average across sources. Adult size similarly varies slightly between sources (FishBase: 4.0 cm; LiveAquaria: ~5 cm; ORA: ~4.5 cm); the range 2.5–5.0 cm covers juveniles through maximum reported size.
breedingNotes: Reported to spawn in captivity; protogynous hermaphrodites with the dominant individual transitioning to male. Captive-bred specimens are commercially available through specialist suppliers. Spawning has been documented as early as roughly 100 days post-hatching under aquaculture conditions. Home aquarium breeding is possible but has been described as somewhat challenging relative to other small reef fish.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for tank size (10 gal), temperature (72–78°F), salinity (1.020–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), dKH (8–12), care level (Easy), and diet notes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4.0 cm TL), temperature range (24.9–28°C wild habitat), taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes), and native range (Western Central Atlantic). Temperature min is higher than LiveAquaria's listing; final range averaged across both sources.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4 cm), minimum tank volume (50 L), salinity (~1.026 SG), reef-safe status, and schooling/grouping behavior. Minimum tank size averaged between LiveAquaria (38 L) and ReefApp (50 L) then biased upward to 75 L to accommodate schooling groups.

additional.name: ORA (Oceans, Reefs & Aquariums)
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~1.75 in / ~4.45 cm), protogynous hermaphroditism, schooling behavior, Caribbean origin, and captive breeding viability.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodites; captive spawning reported in aquaculture and advanced home reef setups. ORA has commercialized captive-bred production. Spawning observed around 100 days post-hatching.
